title: logger.debug overhead in _safe_float is negligible under default INFO log level (benchmarked ~104ns per call when DEBUG is short-circuited)
summary: _safe_float is called ~50+ times directly in compute_all_metrics and recursively by sanitize_metrics over every nested time-series point (returns_series and drawdown_series up to 5000 pts each, sparklines 90 pts, monthly_returns ~120, benchmark_returns up to 5000). When DEBUG is off (default INFO via main.py:22-25 LOG_LEVEL=INFO), each logger.debug call is short-circuited at isEnabledFor — benchmarked 104ns per call including eager type(value).__name__ evaluation. Worst case ~10000 calls per compute_all_metrics × 104ns = ~1ms. The HOT path (valid floats, no NaN/Inf, no coercion failure) skips logger.debug entirely. Net production overhead: sub-millisecond per analytics run. Acceptable.
evidence: Direct timeit benchmark on stdlib logging (Python 3.12): 104.4ms/1M iterations = 104ns each for logger.debug with DEBUG disabled, incl. type().__name__ eager-eval cost of ~30ns. main.py:23 confirms default LOG_LEVEL='INFO'. sanitize_metrics call sites = 3 (metrics.py:649, portfolio.py:299, portfolio.py:1055); none in tight loops.
fix: None required. The DEBUG-level placement (not WARNING) is correct given the call frequency — promoting to WARNING would flood Railway.

title: DEBUG log-flood risk if ops bumps LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG: ~1250+ _safe_float DEBUG lines per analytics run from sanitize_metrics traversing sparse-NaN time-series
summary: When ops flips LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG (per main.py:21 'lets ops bump to DEBUG without a code change') to investigate something specific, _safe_float DEBUG calls become non-trivially loud. For a strategy with 5000-pt returns_series + 5000-pt drawdown_series + benchmark_returns + sparklines, with ~5% NaN rate during sparse early dates, that's ~1250 _safe_float DEBUG lines PER compute_all_metrics run, repeated in portfolio.py's two sanitize_metrics call sites. Signal-to-noise for ops grep is poor when DEBUG is on. Cost when DEBUG IS enabled: ~50us/emission × 1250 = ~60ms per compute_all_metrics; across ~1000 strategies daily = ~1min total daily overhead — fine fleetwise, but the LOG flood drowns the targeted DEBUG signal ops was trying to inspect.
evidence: main.py:22-25 documents LOG_LEVEL=DEBUG as the supported escape hatch. Time-series cap is 5000 (services/transforms.py:241). sanitize_metrics recurses over per-point {date, value} dicts (metrics.py:213-219); each NaN value triggers _safe_float's new DEBUG line.
fix: Optional follow-up — gate the _safe_float DEBUG with a finer-grained flag (e.g., METRICS_SAFE_FLOAT_DEBUG env var) OR drop the DEBUG log on the NaN/Inf-on-known-float path entirely (the calling-site WARNINGs already cover the qs.stats-failed operator-triage need; _safe_float's NaN handling on already-coerced floats from sanitize_metrics is documented-normal, not failure). DEFERRED — out of PR scope; document the trade-off in the docstring so a future ops who flips DEBUG is not surprised by the volume.

title: exc_info=True traceback formatting is one-shot per qs.stats failure path, not amplified — ~50us per emission, sub-millisecond per compute_all_metrics
summary: 11 new WARNING+exc_info=True sites in compute_all_metrics. All fire in the FAILURE branch of single try/except blocks wrapping single qs.stats / np.percentile / pandas-method calls. None are in loops. Per-call cost of logger.warning(..., exc_info=True): traceback.format_exception walks ~5-10 stack frames + emits to the configured handler (~50-500us per emission depending on handler I/O). For a strategy where the documented pinned-quantstats gini AttributeError fires, that's exactly ONE WARNING per compute_all_metrics — ~500us added per run. Fleetwide at ~1000 runs/day = ~0.5s extra daily compute. Negligible.
evidence: All 11 new WARNINGs are in `except Exception as exc:` blocks of single-call try blocks (L348/356, L376/390/398/406/414/424/434/444/452, L473). No `for` or `while` loop wraps any of them. Each compute_all_metrics call = at most one shot per scalar.
fix: None required.

title: Bybit branch single-try wrapping RPC + 200-item loop + ISO-conversion loop does NOT amplify WARNING count (try/except exits at FIRST raise, not per-iteration)
summary: The Bybit try at L593-L618 wraps three things: the private_get_v5_position_closed_pnl RPC, the for-loop over up to 200 items, and the for-loop over daily_pnl converting timestamps. Concern check: if N items all have malformed createdTime, do we emit N WARNINGs each with full exc_info traceback? No — Python's try/except exits the loop at the FIRST raise. Only ONE WARNING fires per fetch_daily_pnl invocation regardless of how many items are bad. The for-loop body at L609-613 has no nested try, so the first ValueError on int() or fromtimestamp() escapes to the outer except and emits one WARNING.
evidence: exchange.py:609-613 has no inner try/except in the per-entry conversion loop. test_exchange.py:2859-2910 confirms the contract by mocking the first item as malformed and asserting a single 'Bybit closed_pnl' WARNING fires.
fix: None today. Optional follow-up: tighten test to assert len(matching) == 1 (currently asserts non-empty `matching`) to pin the single-warning contract against a future refactor that moves the WARNING into the loop. The existing assertion is sufficient for the current implementation.

title: No N+1 introduced: all new try/except wrap EXISTING single calls. No retry-on-failure loops. No GIL/lock additions. No long-lived exception chains.
summary:
Cross-cutting confirmation that the sweep did NOT introduce performance regressions via common anti-patterns:
1. N+1 / fan-out: NO — each new try wraps a single existing call; no batched-insert was decomposed into per-row calls.
2. Retry-on-failure: NO — grep across both diffs for `retry|backoff|sleep|wait` returns empty (no new retry primitives).
3. GIL / lock contention: NO — no `with lock:` or threading primitives added.
4. Memory / exception chains: each `as exc` captures the exception during the WARNING dispatch; CPython releases the traceback at end-of-except. `exc_info=True` saves sys.exc_info() into the LogRecord, which the handler emits and discards. No `raise X from Y` chain that would hold __cause__/__context__ references long-term.
5. Eager arg evaluation: `returns_len_for_log` hoisted ONCE at L346, reused by all 11 WARNINGs — not recomputed per-WARNING.
evidence: `git diff origin/main...HEAD -- analytics-service/services/{metrics,exchange}.py | grep -E '(retry|backoff|sleep|wait |for |while )' -i` shows no new retry/loop primitives. No `with lock:` introduced. `returns_len_for_log = len(returns) if returns is not None else None` at metrics.py:346 is computed once.
fix: None required.

title: sanitize_metrics is invoked ONCE per compute_all_metrics — not in a hot loop. Recursive cost over 5000-pt time-series is unchanged (only the per-NaN DEBUG line is new).
summary:
Fleet-impact check: does the sweep make sanitize_metrics N times more expensive? No.
- Call sites: services/metrics.py:649 (one per compute_all_metrics), routers/portfolio.py:299 + 1055 (one per portfolio analytics request). NOT inside any for/while loop.
- Per-call cost: O(total_floats_in_payload). Worst-case payload (5000-pt returns_series + 5000-pt drawdown_series + 90-pt sparklines + ~120-pt monthly_returns + small dicts) ~10000 _safe_float invocations. Each dominated by float() + isnan/isinf check (~500ns). DEBUG-log adds ~104ns IF DEBUG is on (~0ns short-circuit otherwise). Net: ~5ms per sanitize_metrics, unchanged vs main in production INFO config.
evidence: grep 'sanitize_metrics' across analytics-service shows 3 production call sites (none in loops). Series cap is 5000 (transforms.py:241).
fix: None required.

title: Test-suite added ~19 new tests (~13 in test_exchange + ~6 in test_metrics); each ~10ms with monkeypatch + mock. CI cost increase ~200ms — well under any threshold.
summary:
Test perf check: do the new regression tests slow CI? No.
- test_metrics.py: ~6 new tests using monkeypatch.setattr to swap qs.stats methods or np.percentile / pd.Series.skew/kurtosis/mean — each test runs compute_all_metrics once on golden_returns (a small fixture). ~50-100ms each = ~600ms total.
- test_exchange.py: ~13 new tests in TestFetchDailyPnlBybitFailLoud + TestFetchDailyPnlBinanceFailLoud using AsyncMock / MagicMock — each test is a single coroutine call, ~10-20ms = ~200ms total.
- Total CI cost increase: <1s. Negligible.
evidence: Test files reviewed: monkeypatch.setattr is O(1), AsyncMock side_effect raises immediately, golden_returns fixture is presumably small (no slow setup detected in spot-checks).
fix: None required.

title: CREATE INDEX on portfolio_analytics without CONCURRENTLY blocks all writers
summary: `CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_portfolio_analytics_computing ON portfolio_analytics (...)` takes a full ACCESS EXCLUSIVE-equivalent lock on the table for the index build. Until the build completes, every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on portfolio_analytics blocks — including the live `_compute_portfolio_analytics` INSERTs from the Railway analytics worker and the cron tick.
evidence: portfolio_analytics is an append-only history table (per comment in same file referencing H-0573 — 'next compute attempt INSERTs a fresh row per the append-only contract') and is written by analytics-service/routers/portfolio.py:360 (insert) and the per-portfolio compute cron in routers/cron.py:552. Migration 20260407075303_portfolio_intelligence.sql line 134 already established a base index `idx_portfolio_analytics_latest ON portfolio_analytics (portfolio_id, computed_at DESC)`; adding the new partial variant without CONCURRENTLY repeats a B-tree build on the same key columns under an exclusive lock instead of using SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE. The migration also does NOT set `lock_timeout`, so the lock acquisition itself can queue indefinitely behind a long-running compute.
fix: Move the `CREATE INDEX` out of the wrapping migration tx and use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS idx_portfolio_analytics_computing ON portfolio_analytics (portfolio_id, computed_at DESC) WHERE computation_status = 'computing';` in a separate migration file with no surrounding BEGIN/COMMIT (CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a tx block). Supabase migration runner wraps files — split this into its own file. Alternatively, since the partial WHERE predicate only matches rows with status='computing' (likely << 1000 even on a hot system), an EXCLUSIVE-lock build will finish fast on a small partial set — but you must still SET lock_timeout = '5s' so a hung lock acquisition aborts instead of queueing forever.

title: ALTER TABLE portfolio_analytics ADD COLUMN missing lock_timeout
summary: `ALTER TABLE portfolio_analytics ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS data_quality JSONB` (no DEFAULT) is a metadata-only operation in PG 11+ (no table rewrite), but it still requires ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock acquisition. Without `SET lock_timeout`, the migration can queue behind a long-running analytics compute (1-3 min per portfolio per the stuck-row-reaper file's stale_threshold comment) and block every read+write on portfolio_analytics until the long tx releases.
evidence: Every other migration in this PR (20260516160000 through 20260516160800) follows the convention `BEGIN; SET lock_timeout = '5s';` to bound lock-acquisition latency. This migration deviates with no transaction wrapper at all. Compare to the same PR's 160000_data_deletion_requests_state_check.sql L20-L21 which sets lock_timeout = '5s' before ALTER TABLE.
fix: Wrap the migration body in `BEGIN; SET lock_timeout = '5s'; ... COMMIT;` to match the codebase convention. Also worth: `SET statement_timeout = '30s';` so the whole migration aborts if anything pathological happens during apply.

title: Stuck-row-reaper migration also missing lock_timeout — second lock-acquisition exposure
summary: Same lock_timeout omission as 20260516121000. The file has no `BEGIN; SET lock_timeout = '5s';` wrapper, so the `CREATE INDEX` lock-acquisition can queue behind a long-running compute. Coupled with the HIGH finding above (CONCURRENTLY missing), this compounds: not only is the index build taking an exclusive lock, the acquisition can wait arbitrarily long.
evidence: File has no explicit BEGIN/SET/COMMIT — relies on Supabase's implicit per-file tx wrapper. The audit migrations 160000-160800 explicitly set lock_timeout = '5s' even though they run inside Supabase's wrapper, because the EXPLICIT SET is what bounds the lock-acquisition cost.
fix: Add `BEGIN; SET lock_timeout = '5s';` at top of file and `COMMIT;` at end — but note this conflicts with CONCURRENTLY (which cannot run inside a tx block). The clean split is: one migration for the FUNCTION + COMMENT (transactional, with lock_timeout), one for the `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` (non-transactional, separate file).

title: COUNT(*) in visibility helper should use EXISTS for early exit
summary: `_assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator` runs `SELECT COUNT(*) INTO v_org_member_count FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = v_org_id` then checks `IF v_org_member_count = 0`. COUNT(*) scans every member row in the index range to compute the total even though we only need to know whether any rows exist (and the very next operation is an EXISTS-style probe for the specific user anyway).
evidence: The trigger fires BEFORE INSERT on match_decisions per row. While the (organization_id, user_id) PK on organization_members covers the query, COUNT(*) reads the full index range for the org. For organizations with hundreds of members, this is wasted IO compared to `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = v_org_id LIMIT 1)` which stops at the first row. Note: match_decisions write volume is low (2 INSERT sites per grep) so impact is small — keeping as LOW.
fix: Replace lines 78-84 with `SELECT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = v_org_id) INTO v_org_has_members; IF NOT v_org_has_members THEN RETURN TRUE; END IF;`. Even better: collapse into a single query `RETURN (NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = v_org_id)) OR EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM organization_members WHERE organization_id = v_org_id AND user_id = p_allocator_id);` so only one round-trip happens for the membership branch.

title: Visibility trigger calls SECURITY DEFINER helper per row — measurable overhead in batch INSERTs
summary: `_match_decisions_visibility_check` is a BEFORE INSERT FOR EACH ROW trigger that calls `_assert_strategy_visible_to_allocator` (SECURITY DEFINER, STABLE). Each call switches role context, sets the search_path, runs 2-3 lookups, and unwinds. commit_scenario_batch's loop (20260426131720_commit_scenario_batch_rpc.sql L106) inserts rows one-at-a-time, so the trigger fires N times per batch.
evidence: BEFORE INSERT trigger + SECDEF helper has overhead vs. a non-SECDEF helper. The helper is correctly marked STABLE but the function-invocation cost is per-row, not amortized. For typical batches (1-50 allocator-scenario diffs), this is sub-ms and not a bottleneck — keeping as LOW. The architectural choice (trigger vs inline RPC check) is reasonable for defense-in-depth: trigger catches future writers, not just commit_scenario_batch.
fix: No fix required for current scope. If batch sizes grow >>100, consider moving the visibility check inline into commit_scenario_batch's loop where it can be combined with the strategy ownership lookup the RPC already does (`SELECT status FROM strategies WHERE id = (v_diff->>'strategy_id')::uuid` at L153). One extra JOIN to organization_members would handle both checks in a single query per diff.

title: _assert_retention_columns REVOKEd from service_role contradicts canary-cron callability comment
summary: Helper is REVOKEd from PUBLIC, anon, authenticated, AND service_role. The comment block at L13-L15 / L75-L80 says the helper is 'intended to be re-callable from a future canary cron if/when one is built' — but the typical Supabase cron pattern runs the body as service_role. If a future cron tries to invoke as service_role, it will fail with insufficient_privilege. Not a perf issue today; logged because the comment claims re-callability that the GRANT posture forbids.
evidence: Line 83: `REVOKE ALL ON FUNCTION public._assert_retention_columns() FROM PUBLIC, anon, authenticated, service_role;` — vs the COMMENT at L75-L80 'intended to be re-callable from a future canary cron'.
fix: Either (a) `GRANT EXECUTE ON FUNCTION public._assert_retention_columns() TO service_role;` to match the documented intent of canary-cron callability, or (b) update the COMMENT to clarify 'callable only from migration apply-time as superuser; canary cron will require a future GRANT'. Pure documentation/posture nit — no current operational impact.

title: Unbounded ctval_drift_warnings list during replay before final 50-entry cap
summary: _compute_daily_equity appends to ctval_drift_warnings on every perp trade where cost/price drift sits in the 1-5% warn band. The list has no in-loop cap; only the final _emit_audit at line 1842 caps it at [:50]. For a 2-year OKX backfill with up to 250k trades (per _fetch_trades_paginated_one_pass ceiling), a noisy account with many borderline-drift trades could accumulate thousands of 5-key dicts (~10-20MB transient memory) for ~50 ever surfaced. Frequency: once per perp trade in the drift-warn band, per allocator backfill.
evidence: analytics-service/services/equity_reconstruction.py:957 (append) vs 1842 (cap)
fix: Cap the in-loop list at, e.g., 200 entries (4x the audit cap to keep a healthy sample) by guarding the append with `if len(ctval_drift_warnings) < 200:` before the dict construction, so worst-case payload stays bounded regardless of trade volume.

title: Redundant str(inst_type).upper() comparisons at call site duplicate work _resolve_perp_amt_base already did
summary: The post-call audit gate (line 926-932) re-evaluates `str(inst_type).upper() in _OKX_LINEAR_INST_TYPES` and `str(venue).lower() != 'okx'` for every perp trade, after _resolve_perp_amt_base already made the same decision. With up to 250k trades per backfill this is ~500k redundant str-allocations + dict hashes. Tiny per trade, but pure waste on the hot replay loop. Frequency: per perp trade in _compute_daily_equity.
evidence: analytics-service/services/equity_reconstruction.py:929 (caller) and analytics-service/services/equity_reconstruction.py:306 (callee)
fix: Have _resolve_perp_amt_base return a 4th element `applied_okx_table_gate: bool` (True iff inst_type+venue passed the gate), and replace the line 928-930 string conversions with a single boolean check. Or normalise inst_type/venue once at the top of the trade-event branch (line 906) and reuse the upper/lower strings downstream.

title: events_by_date sort uses int(e.get('timestamp') or 0) Python-side for every event
summary: For each ISO day in events_by_date, the code re-sorts the list with `key=lambda e: int(e.get('timestamp') or 0)`. Trades from ccxt already arrive timestamp-ascending out of paginated fetch_my_trades. With up to 250k trades distributed across 730 days, this O(events_per_day log events_per_day) re-sort is wasted CPU work — and it allocates a fresh lambda + int conversion per element. Frequency: per day with >1 event, per backfill.
evidence: analytics-service/services/equity_reconstruction.py:810-811
fix: Either (a) trust ccxt's chronological ordering and skip the sort entirely (add a comment + a one-shot is-sorted assertion in dev), or (b) sort the flat trade/deposit/withdrawal lists once before bucketing into events_by_date — buckets inherit chronological order for free, eliminating the per-day re-sort. The pre-bucket sort is one O(N log N) pass vs daily O(K log K) for every populated day.

title: _purge_allocator_equity_snapshots fetches full row representation on bulk delete
summary: supabase-py's `.delete()` defaults to `Prefer: return=representation`, so the purge round-trips every deleted snapshot row back over the wire just so `_result_row_count` can `len(res.data)` it. For an allocator with 730 daily snapshots × breakdown blobs (~few KB each) that's a ~1-5MB payload per single-key reconstruction purge. The diff's docstring acknowledges PostgREST's response-size limit. Frequency: once per single-key reconstruct (covers the recovery path users hit).
evidence: analytics-service/services/equity_reconstruction.py:1314-1323
fix: Add `.select('id', count='exact')` or `.execute(count='exact')` (whichever the supabase-py version supports) to the delete builder so PostgREST returns `Prefer: count=exact, return=minimal`. Then `_result_row_count` falls through to `res.count` without round-tripping representations. Reduces bandwidth + JSON parse cost by ~99% on this path.

title: Rebuild step's '.next/cache → incremental' comment is wrong; rebuild is full cold (~47-50s observed, not the claimed 30-60s incremental)
summary: The new 'Rebuild Next.js with real test-Supabase env' step claims (in its own header comment, lines 657-660) that '`.next/cache` (the incremental webpack/Turbopack store) is left intact so the rebuild is incremental on top of the placeholder run.' That's factually wrong. `.next/cache` does NOT exist on the e2e runner when this step starts: (1) the upload-artifact step at line 197-209 explicitly excludes `.next/cache/**` from `nextjs-build`; (2) the e2e job (line 504 onward) only restores Playwright browsers via actions/cache and does NOT restore the `.next/cache` actions/cache that frontend-build (line 170-176) populates. So the rebuild starts from a fresh node_modules + a `.next/` containing only stripped manifests + the placeholder static chunks (about to be deleted), with `.next/cache` empty. That matches the observed runtime: across 3 runs (fd8de279 + its PR head a76a995a + the prior PR push 89103523), the rebuild step measured 47s, 50s, 47s — at the upper end of the documented 30-60s range, consistent with a cold Next.js build, NOT an incremental one. Net effect on `main` CI wall-clock: e2e job went from ~89s avg pre-merge (3 successful seed-gated runs) to ~129s avg post-merge (4 successful seed-gated runs), a ~40-45s regression. Total CI run time (push events to `main`): ~196s avg pre-merge → ~239s avg post-merge, +22%. e2e is the long pole, so this slips straight to wall-clock.
evidence: GH Actions job-step timings via `gh api repos/AI-Isaiah/quantalyze/actions/runs/{id}/jobs`: pre-merge run 25965271425 (1889677f) e2e=100s (no rebuild step); post-merge run 25965854338 (fd8de279) e2e=145s with rebuild=50s; pre-merge run 25964476664 (ef93edea) e2e=95s; post-merge run 25966174204 (a24a2b4a) e2e=117s; post-merge run 25966417892 (d0edf71d) e2e=127s; post-merge run 25966952364 (9af2b5da) e2e=128s. Workflow source: .github/workflows/ci.yml:197-209 (upload excludes .next/cache/**), :504-550 (e2e job has no .next/cache restore), :640-669 (rebuild step with wrong comment).
fix: Two options, increasing-impact: (a) Cheap: fix the misleading comment — say 'starts from clean state; no `.next/cache` to incremental-build against' so future readers don't trust a guarantee that isn't there. (b) Real fix: add an `actions/cache` restore for `.next/cache` in the e2e job, keyed on the SAME key as the frontend-build job's cache step (line 170-176). On a warm cache the rebuild becomes genuinely incremental and the observed 47-50s drops to ~20-30s (just NEXT_PUBLIC_* re-bake + manifest rewrite). The cache is read-only restore from the e2e side (frontend-build is the writer), so no cross-job race. Add restore-keys with the prefix-only fallback to handle the case where frontend-build's cache step hashed src/** to a different bucket.

title: Total CI wall-clock regression on main: +22% (~196s → ~239s) post-merge, driven entirely by e2e rebuild
summary: Aggregate timing comparison across recent push-to-main runs (10 pre-merge vs 4 post-merge, all conclusion=success): pre-merge avg 196s (range 177-214s); post-merge avg 239s (range 222-257s). All other jobs are unchanged within noise: frontend-build (npm run build) is 49s pre and 49s post; frontend-typecheck 30-39s both sides; frontend-test (slowest shard) 95-110s both sides; python 116-139s both sides. The entire regression is concentrated in e2e (89s avg → 129s avg, +40s). e2e is on the critical path (it `needs: frontend-build, frontend-typecheck, frontend-test`), so this slips straight to total wall-clock. For comparison, the prior /simplify pass on this PR (per the cluster-gate runbook) cited '~30-60s' rebuild cost — actual is 47-50s i.e. lower-bound-violated against the optimistic case, but within stated range. The cost is real per-merge, not amortized.
evidence: `gh run list --workflow=ci.yml --branch=main` durations: pre-merge (before 2026-05-16T15:34:57Z): 214s, 177s, 212s, 177s, 178s, 206s, 211s, 203s, 193s, 211s, 205s, 181s, 190s; post-merge: 257s (fd8de279), 222s (a24a2b4a), 235s (d0edf71d), 244s (9af2b5da). Per-job from `gh api` show e2e is the sole regressing job.
fix: Either accept the +22% wall-clock as the cost of closing C-0293(c), or apply the .next/cache restore fix in the prior finding to reclaim ~20-30s of the regression. Other options the PR's own cluster-gate runbook explicitly rejected ('stop uploading the artifact at all', which would push the cost back into e2e but save the artifact upload step ~9s) are still on the table if the rebuild proves persistently slow.

title: SHA-pinning produced zero cold-start regression — first-party actions cache identically when pinned by SHA
summary: The hypothesis that SHA pinning would block action upgrades and cause cold-start regressions on the runner did NOT materialize. Step-level timings for frontend-build's `actions/setup-node` and `actions/checkout` steps are identical pre-/post-merge within noise: setup-node 2-5s pre, 5s post; checkout 1s pre, 2s post; npm ci 17-18s pre, 16-17s post. Looking up the v4/v5 mutable tags currently shows that `actions/setup-node@v4` resolves to 49933ea5288caeca8642d1e84afbd3f7d6820020 — the EXACT SHA the PR pinned to. Same for setup-python@v5. So pinning didn't downgrade, and GitHub-hosted runners' embedded action cache handles SHA-keyed actions just like tag-keyed ones. No regression to flag here; this finding is informational evidence that closes the audit hypothesis.
evidence: Pre-merge run 25964476664 frontend-build steps (setup-node=5s, checkout=1s, npm ci=18s, build=49s); post-merge run 25965854338 frontend-build steps (setup-node=5s, checkout=2s, npm ci=16s, build=49s). `gh api repos/actions/setup-node/git/ref/tags/v4` returns the same SHA the workflow pinned.
fix: None — pin strategy is performance-neutral.

title: Cache keys preserved across the SHA-pin migration; no thrashing
summary: Every `actions/cache` invocation kept its existing `key` and `restore-keys`. The cache keys are entirely user-controlled strings keyed on hashFiles() of source/lock/config files, NOT on the action version. Bumping from `actions/cache@v4` to `actions/cache@v4.3.0` (same major) does not affect cache-bucket addressing. Observed in step timings: 'Cache .next/cache' = 1-2s pre, 2s post (cache hit both sides); 'Cache Playwright browsers' = 3-10s pre, 5s post (hit both sides). No cache invalidation event occurred at the merge boundary. Note: `actions/cache@v5` would silently change cache-key compression and could invalidate buckets, but the PR did not bump major.
evidence: Pre-merge run 25964855064 e2e 'Cache Playwright browsers' = 3s; post-merge run 25965854338 e2e 'Cache Playwright browsers' = 5s. frontend-build 'Cache .next/cache' = 1s pre (25965271425), 2s post (25965854338). Source: .github/workflows/ci.yml:56,79,109,171,519 all preserved their `key` expressions.
fix: None — caches are intact. If a future bump goes to actions/cache@v5 (current major), schedule a one-time cache rebuild + monitor the first warm run.

title: Parallel job utilization unchanged — all frontend-* jobs still fan out simultaneously
summary: Inspecting the fd8de279 merge run, all 9 parallel jobs (frontend-typecheck, frontend-lint, frontend-test (1/2/3), frontend-policy, frontend-build, sql-tests, python, secret-scan, docs-link-check) started within the same second (15:35:00-15:35:01).
